findings.title: Hidden external controller can selectively block transfers and sells

best_agent_match.title: A hidden external controller can selectively block transfers and sells

findings.title: External controller fully controls transfer debits and credits, enabling confiscation and hidden minting

best_agent_match.title: Transfer balance changes are fully attacker-controlled, enabling confiscation and hidden minting

findings.title: Anyone can seize 80% of any holder balance through `addLiquidityETH` and route the stolen tokens to arbitrary recipients

best_agent_match.title: Public liquidity bootstrap can seize 80% of any address balance and mint LP to an arbitrary recipient

rejected_candidates.title: Incorrect UniswapV2Router Interface Causes Transfer Failure
rejected_candidates.reason: The contract is not necessarily meant to call a standard Uniswap router here; it deliberately points at a custom contract implementing the spoofed signature. Transfers only always fail if `_router` is set to a real router, so the candidate is inaccurate as a standalone issue and is subsumed by the controller backdoor findings.

rejected_candidates.title: Unlimited Token Approval to Router
rejected_candidates.reason: This is part of the already-critical unrestricted `addLiquidityETH` exploit path. The overbroad approval helps the theft, but it is not a distinct issue from the arbitrary balance seizure captured in F-003.

rejected_candidates.title: External Call Before State Update Enables Manipulation
rejected_candidates.reason: The report describes the symptom, not the root issue. The material vulnerability is that the external controller dictates `subBal` and `addBal` without validation, which is captured in F-002.

rejected_candidates.title: Transfer events can be falsified to hide the real balance changes
rejected_candidates.reason: The misleading event is a direct consequence of the arbitrary debit/credit backdoor in F-002. On its own it is mainly an observability issue and does not warrant a separate protocol-level finding.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Zero Address Check in addLiquidityETH
rejected_candidates.reason: Passing zero addresses is a caller-inflicted configuration error that at worst reverts or self-sabotages the caller's own liquidity setup; it is not a meaningful adversarial vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Block Timestamp Dependency for Deadline
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a generic low-signal timestamp observation on a same-block liquidity call and does not create a realistic exploit of protocol funds or availability.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Return Value Check for ERC20 approve
rejected_candidates.reason: `approve` calls the contract's own internal `_approve`, which does not return a value and updates allowance storage directly. The candidate is technically incorrect.

rejected_candidates.title: Public decode Function Exposes Router Address
rejected_candidates.reason: The encoded router address is already on-chain state; exposing a pure decode helper is not a material vulnerability.
